reasoning:
After a comprehensive review of all major cases involving executives of the targeted indices (S&P 500, FTSE 100, DAX 40, CAC 40, FTSE MIB, Nikkei 225), it is highly unlikely this question resolves positively. Since 2021, no eligible executive has started a prison sentence: Carrie Tolstedt (Wells Fargo) received probation, Rupert Stadler (Volkswagen) received a suspended sentence, and the SMBC Nikko executives were given suspended sentences. High-profile fraudsters who did go to prison, such as Sam Bankman-Fried or Trevor Milton (Nikola), were not executives of companies in these indices during the required timeframe. 

The two main candidates implied by the background text are extremely unlikely to cause a positive resolution before Jan 2026:
1. Wirecard was removed from the DAX in August 2020, missing the "any time in 2021 or later" eligibility window altogether.
2. Martin Winterkorn's trial (VW) is currently scheduled with dates extending into September 2025 and has been repeatedly disrupted by his severe health issues. Given the slowness of German legal proceedings, eventual appeals, and his questionable fitness for prison, it is practically impossible for him to begin serving an actual prison sentence before the January 1, 2026 deadline.

Without any eligible executives currently serving time, and no imminent trials likely to conclude and survive appeals before 2026, the real-world probability is near purely the base rate of an unexpected sudden conviction plus any blind spots, which I place at the 5% floor.
